Just now

Press enter or click to view image in full size

I started preparation for my Tech Interviews. One of the first coding challenges I encountered on platforms like Coderbyte is Codeland Username Validation. Although it looks simple, it tests several important Python concepts in a single problem: string manipulation, regular expressions, conditional statements, and built-in string methods.
Let’s solve it step by step.
Problem Statement
Write a function that returns "true" if a username is valid; otherwise, return "false".
A valid username must satisfy all of the following conditions:

• Be between 4 and 25 characters long.

• Start with a letter.

• Contain only letters, numbers, and underscores (_).

• Not end with an underscore.
Understanding the Requirements
Instead of writing code immediately, let’s translate each requirement and condition individually first into Python. Always break the problem into smaller steps first.
1. Check the Length
The username should have at least 4 characters and at most 25. We can use a comparison parameter here.
4 <= len(strParam) <= 25Python allows chained comparisons, making this very readable.
2. Check the First Character
The username must begin with a letter.
Python provides the built-in function isalpha(), which is used to check whether a string consists only of alphabetical characters. It returns a Boolean value: True if all characters in the string are letters, and False otherwise
strParam[0].isalpha()Examples:
"Shruti".isalpha() # True
"A".isalpha() # True
"9".isalpha() # False
"_".isalpha() # False3. Allow Only Letters, Numbers, and Underscores
This is where regular expressions (regex) become useful.
The Pattern which is perfect here :
^[A-Za-z0-9_]+$Let’s break it down. Don’t be scared — it is not that hard! I am saying identify capital letters A-Z, small letters a-z, numbers 0–9, and underscore _
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Regex Pattern RecognitionThis ensures that every character in the username is in the allowed set.
Example:
import repattern = "^[A-Za-z0-9_]+$"
bool(re.match(pattern, "Shruti_99"))
Output:
True # as Shruti_99 has letters, digit or underscore4. Ensure the Username Doesn’t End with “_”
Python lets us access the last character using negative indexing. The last element is always accessed with -1. Pretty cool, right :)
strParam[-1] != "_"Examples:
"Shruti_"Returns:
False # Because at the end there is a underscorewhile
"Shruti99"Returns:
True # Because no underscore, right?Final Solution: Joining the Pieces
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Time and Space Complexity
Each validation step scans the username at most once.

• Length check: O(1)

• First character check: O(1)

• Last character check: O(1)

• Regular expression match: O(n)
Overall Time Complexity: O(n)
where n is the length of the username.
Overall Space Complexity: O(1)
Key Takeaways
This challenge is a great introduction to combining multiple Python concepts in one solution.
By solving it, you practice:

• String indexing

• Conditional logic

• Regular expressions

• Boolean expressions

• Built-in string methods like isalpha()
While the problem itself is straightforward, the problem-solving method uses breaking a problem into small validation rules and checking each one systematically, and it is a technique you’ll use repeatedly in technical interviews and real-world software development.
Small validation problems like this build the foundation for larger systems such as form validation, authentication, and input sanitization.
Do you know problem-solving will keep your brain healthy! :)
~ Shruti Mandaokar
